2005.assessment:
Gambia had high public debt in 2005, estimated around 85% of GDP.
The country had accumulated significant debt under Jammeh's mismanagement
and previous HIPC debt relief. Fiscal deficits were common as the
government ran expansive spending without adequate revenue.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2006.assessment:
Public debt remained at approximately 80% of GDP in 2006, still
elevated. Some improvement from HIPC completion point benefits.
Fiscal management remained weak under Jammeh.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2007.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 78% of GDP in 2007. The economy's
growth in 2007 helped moderate debt-to-GDP somewhat. Fiscal
discipline remained limited.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2008.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 75% of GDP in 2008. At peak economic
activity, debt-to-GDP declined somewhat but remained elevated.
Fiscal management continued to be poor.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2009.assessment:
Public debt rose to approximately 82% of GDP in 2009 as global
crisis reduced revenues and the government continued deficit spending.
The fiscal situation worsened with the economic downturn.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2010.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 80% of GDP in 2010. Continued high
fiscal deficits maintained debt at elevated levels despite some
economic recovery.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2011.assessment:
Public debt rose to approximately 85% of GDP in 2011 with drought-driven
economic contraction reducing the denominator. Fiscal deficits
continued under Jammeh's governance.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2012.assessment:
Public debt rose to approximately 90% of GDP in 2012 as fiscal
deficits accumulated. Gambia was in debt distress territory with
high domestic and external borrowing under Jammeh.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2013.assessment:
Public debt rose to approximately 95% of GDP in 2013. Gambia's
fiscal position was increasingly unsustainable with high domestic
interest rates and growing external debt.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis

2014.assessment:
Public debt exceeded 100% of GDP in 2014 as the economic contraction
and continued fiscal deficits pushed the debt ratio into very high
territory. Gambia was assessed as in high debt distress by the IMF.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV Consultation 2014

2015.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 110% of GDP in 2015. Gambia was in
a state of fiscal distress with unsustainable debt levels and high
interest payments crowding out public spending. IMF engagement
was difficult given Jammeh's resistance to conditionality.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis 2015

2016.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 115% of GDP in 2016, at peak levels
under Jammeh's rule. Fiscal mismanagement, high defense spending,
and state enterprise losses drove debt to unsustainable levels.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Debt Sustainability Analysis 2016

2017.assessment:
Public debt began declining from peak levels in 2017 as the new
democratic government engaged with the IMF and international creditors.
Barrow's government inherited the accumulated Jammeh debt burden.
Initial debt restructuring discussions began.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV 2017

2018.assessment:
Public debt fell to approximately 100% of GDP in 2018 as the
new government pursued fiscal consolidation and received debt
relief. IMF ECF program helped restore fiscal discipline.
Debt remained very high but trajectory was improving.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Extended Credit Facility 2018

2019.assessment:
Public debt declined to approximately 90% of GDP in 2019 with
fiscal consolidation and economic growth reducing the ratio.
Still very high but showing clear improvement from peak levels.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV 2019

2020.assessment:
Public debt rose to approximately 95% of GDP in 2020 as COVID-19
caused economic contraction and the government borrowed to finance
emergency spending. Fiscal gains of 2018-2019 were partially reversed.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV 2020

2021.assessment:
Public debt fell to approximately 88% of GDP in 2021 as the economy
recovered strongly. IMF program continued to support fiscal discipline.
Debt trajectory was improving.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV 2021

2022.assessment:
Public debt fell to approximately 78% of GDP in 2022 as strong
economic growth and continued fiscal consolidation reduced the ratio.
The trajectory showed sustained improvement from Jammeh-era peaks.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV 2022

2023.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 76% of GDP in 2023. The IMF noted
that the overall debt distress risk remained high but the ratio
was declining. Continued fiscal consolidation underway.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Article IV 2023; Debt Sustainability Analysis

2024.assessment:
Public debt at approximately 72% of GDP in 2024. IMF reported this
was approximately 5 percentage points higher than previously projected
due to exchange rate depreciation. Overall debt distress risk remained
high but the ratio continued declining from peak levels.

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia Country Report 2025

2025.assessment:
Public debt expected to continue declining in 2025 as IMF projects
a 18.8 percentage point reduction from 2024 to 2029. Still
elevated but improving trajectory.

sources.citation: IMF projection for Gambia debt trajectory

2005.assessment:
Gambia's GDP per capita was $619 in 2005, representing 72.1% of the
2008 peak of $858. Growth was moderate in the 2000s driven by tourism
and remittances. The economy was small and heavily aid-dependent.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2006.assessment:
GDP per capita $616 in 2006, essentially flat from 2005, representing
71.8% of peak. The economy was stagnant under Jammeh's mismanagement
despite modest tourism receipts.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2007.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $725 in 2007 (84.5% of peak), a significant
improvement driven by strong tourism and good agricultural seasons.
The global commodity price boom also supported economic activity.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2008.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $858 in 2008, the peak year for the series.
This represented the height of Gambia's pre-crisis prosperity
in current USD terms, supported by tourism, remittances, and
modest re-export trade.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2009.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to $772 in 2009 (90% of peak) following the global
financial crisis impact on tourism and remittances. The falloff was
moderate as Gambia's small size limited exposure to global capital markets.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2010.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered to $797 in 2010 (92.9% of peak). Tourism
began recovering from the 2009 global financial crisis impact.
Agricultural performance was adequate.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2011.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to $705 in 2011 (82.2% of peak) due to drought
affecting agriculture and continued challenges in the tourism sector.
Economic mismanagement under Jammeh contributed to weak performance.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2012.assessment:
GDP per capita at $687 in 2012 (80.1% of peak). Continued drought and
poor harvest in 2011-2012. International aid reduced following human
rights concerns. Economy remained stagnant under Jammeh's rule.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2013.assessment:
GDP per capita fell further to $647 in 2013 (75.4% of peak). The
economy continued to underperform with erratic agricultural output
and declining foreign direct investment due to the hostile business
environment under Jammeh.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2014.assessment:
GDP per capita fell sharply to $562 in 2014 (65.5% of peak). A severe
drought, Ebola fears reducing tourism (despite no Ebola cases in Gambia),
and economic mismanagement all contributed to a significant decline.
This was the worst year economically since the early 2000s.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2015.assessment:
GDP per capita partially recovered to $612 in 2015 (71.3% of peak).
Tourism recovered somewhat from the Ebola-related fears. Agricultural
output improved. However, the economy remained structurally weak.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2016.assessment:
GDP per capita at $641 in 2016 (74.7% of peak). The economy showed
modest recovery. Political uncertainty from the December electoral
crisis negatively impacted business confidence. Tourism declined as
the crisis unfolded.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2017.assessment:
GDP per capita at $632 in 2017 (73.7% of peak). The political transition
year saw economic uncertainty during the transition crisis (Jan 2017),
followed by improved confidence after Barrow's consolidation. Tourism
began recovering from political uncertainty.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2018.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $683 in 2018 (79.6% of peak). The democratic
transition boosted international confidence, donor support, and
tourism. The economy showed meaningful improvement under the
new democratic government.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2019.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $723 in 2019 (84.3% of peak). Continued
improvement under democratic governance with strong tourism recovery,
increased foreign aid flows, and improved investment climate.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2020.assessment:
GDP per capita fell to $704 in 2020 (82.1% of peak) due to COVID-19
pandemic. Tourism collapsed as international travel halted. Remittances
held up somewhat but the economy contracted. The impact was significant
given tourism's importance to the Gambian economy.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2021.assessment:
GDP per capita recovered strongly to $763 in 2021 (88.9% of peak).
Tourism began recovering, remittances remained strong, and the
democratic government attracted additional donor support. COVID
impacts began to fade.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2022.assessment:
GDP per capita rose to $804 in 2022 (93.7% of peak). Strong recovery
with tourism reaching pre-COVID levels and good remittance flows.
The economy was approaching its historical peak despite global
inflationary pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2023.assessment:
GDP per capita reached $844 in 2023 (98.4% of peak), nearly reaching
the 2008 nominal USD peak. Strong tourism, remittances, and democratic
governance dividends contributed. The economy was experiencing a
sustained recovery under democratic governance.

sources.citation: World Bank, GDP per capita (current US$), The Gambia

2024.assessment:
GDP per capita estimated around $893-989 in 2024, likely surpassing
the 2008 nominal peak. IMF projected $989 for 2024. Continued tourism
recovery and strong remittances drove growth. Stored as 100%+ of
previous peak as the economy surpassed earlier nominal highs.

sources.citation: IMF, World Economic Outlook 2024, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
GDP per capita expected to continue growing in 2025 under democratic
governance. Likely above 2008 nominal peak given growth trajectory.

sources.citation: IMF trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Inflation was 5.0% in 2005, moderate for a West African economy.
The Gambia's CFA-adjacent economy and peg history kept inflation
relatively contained. Food price pressures were the primary driver.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Inflation fell to 2.1% in 2006, a low rate reflecting stable commodity
prices and modest monetary expansion. The Dalasi remained relatively
stable.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Inflation rose to 5.4% in 2007, driven by rising global food and
fuel prices beginning to affect the Gambian economy. Still within
moderate range.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Inflation at 4.5% in 2008. While global food and oil prices spiked
to record highs in 2008, Gambia's inflation remained moderate due
to government price controls on basic commodities and subsidies.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Inflation remained stable at 4.6% in 2009 despite the global
financial crisis. The economy's relative isolation from global
financial markets limited crisis transmission.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Inflation at 5.0% in 2010, moderate and broadly stable. Food price
pressures continued but were managed. The Dalasi remained in its
typical range.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Inflation at 4.8% in 2011. Global commodity prices rose but Gambia's
inflation remained moderate. The drought affecting agriculture created
some food price pressures.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Inflation at 4.6% in 2012, moderate and relatively stable. The
economy continued under Jammeh's mismanagement but monetary policy
kept inflation in single digits.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Inflation at 5.2% in 2013. Moderate inflation driven by food
prices and Dalasi depreciation pressures. The economy continued
underperforming under Jammeh.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Inflation rose to 6.3% in 2014. The economic decline from drought
and Ebola fears created supply-side pressures. Currency depreciation
added to import price inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Inflation at 6.8% in 2015, rising slightly. Continued food price
pressures and currency depreciation. The economy recovered modestly
but inflation remained elevated above earlier years.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Inflation at 7.2% in 2016. Political uncertainty from the December
electoral crisis began affecting the Dalasi. Continued food price
pressures and import dependency drove inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Inflation at 8.0% in 2017, elevated partly due to transition-related
economic disruption early in the year. The Dalasi came under depreciation
pressure. Post-transition economic recovery began in the second half
but full-year inflation remained elevated.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
Inflation fell to 6.5% in 2018 as the new democratic government
improved macroeconomic management. The Dalasi stabilized somewhat
with increased donor support. Food prices remained a key driver.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Inflation at 7.1% in 2019. Food prices and currency pressures
maintained moderate inflation. The economy was growing but inflation
remained in the 7% range typical for the country.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Inflation fell to 5.9% in 2020. Reduced demand from COVID-19 crisis
dampened inflationary pressures. Tourism collapse reduced some import
demand. Overall inflation remained moderate.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Inflation rose to 7.4% in 2021 as economic recovery brought demand-pull
pressures and global supply chain disruptions pushed up import costs.
Food inflation was a primary driver.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Inflation surged to 11.5% in 2022 driven by global food and energy
price spikes following Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Gambia as a
food-importing nation was significantly affected by global commodity
price rises.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Inflation reached 17.0% in 2023, the highest rate in the series.
Continued global food price pressures, Dalasi depreciation, and
supply constraints drove inflation to uncomfortable levels. The IMF
engaged with the government on managing the high-inflation environment.

sources.citation: World Bank, Inflation data, The Gambia; IMF Article IV 2023

2024.assessment:
Inflation moderated to 11.6% in 2024 as global commodity prices
eased somewhat. Still elevated compared to the pre-2022 period
but showing signs of return toward more moderate levels.

sources.citation: IMF, World Economic Outlook 2024, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Inflation expected to continue moderating in 2025 as global commodity
pressures ease and IMF program takes effect. Likely to remain elevated
relative to pre-2022 levels.

sources.citation: IMF trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia had moderate trade openness in 2005 with trade around 65% of GDP.
As a re-export hub for the region and a tourism destination, Gambia
historically had relatively high trade-to-GDP ratios. Imports dominated
given Gambia's limited domestic production capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Trade openness remained at moderate levels in 2006. Gambia's position
as a transit economy maintained relatively open trade flows relative
to its GDP size.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Trade openness at approximately 68% in 2007, supported by tourism
receipts (services exports) and re-export trade in the sub-region.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Trade openness reached approximately 70% of GDP in 2008 at the economic
peak, supported by strong tourism and regional re-export trade.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Trade openness fell with the global financial crisis impact on tourism.
Services exports (tourism) declined, reducing overall trade-to-GDP ratio.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Trade openness modestly recovered to around 63% in 2010 as tourism
and re-export trade partially rebounded from the global crisis.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Trade openness fell to approximately 58% in 2011 due to drought
impacts and lower re-export trade. The economy's trade integration
declined during the difficult period.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 55% in 2012. International isolation
from Jammeh's policies and growing tensions with donors began
affecting Gambia's trade integration.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Trade openness approximately 52% in 2013. Gambia's international
isolation grew with the Commonwealth withdrawal. Trade with
the sub-region declined as business confidence fell.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Trade openness fell to approximately 48% in 2014 as the economy
contracted. Ebola fears reduced tourism (services exports) and
overall trade integration declined.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Trade openness modestly recovered to approximately 50% in 2015
as tourism partially rebounded from Ebola fears.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Trade openness fell to approximately 42% in 2016, near the historical
minimum (38% in 2016 per some estimates). Political uncertainty from
the December electoral crisis further dampened trade activity.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Trade openness began recovering modestly in 2017 following the
democratic transition. Improved international relations led to
some recovery in trade flows and donor-financed imports.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
Trade openness improved to approximately 52% in 2018 as tourism
recovered, aid flows increased, and the business environment improved
under democratic governance.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Trade openness continued improving to around 55% in 2019 with
strong tourism performance and growing import activity from
economic recovery.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Trade openness fell sharply in 2020 with COVID-19. Tourism exports
collapsed as international travel halted. Trade-to-GDP ratio fell
substantially despite continued import activity for basic goods.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Trade openness recovered to approximately 50% in 2021 as tourism
began recovering and overall economic activity rebounded.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Trade openness improved to approximately 55% in 2022 with strong
tourism recovery and higher import costs due to global commodity
price inflation.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Trade openness at approximately 57% in 2023. Strong tourism and
growing trade flows reflect improving economic integration. The
World Bank reported trade at approximately 42.6% GDP for recent years
in one estimate, though tourism adds significantly to services trade.

sources.citation: World Bank, Trade % of GDP, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Trade openness expected to be at moderate levels in 2024 with
continued tourism recovery and stable trade flows.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2025.assessment:
Trade openness expected to remain moderate in 2025. Gambia's open
small economy maintains significant trade dependence.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia had no reliable formal unemployment statistics in 2005. The
economy was predominantly informal, subsistence agriculture, and
small-scale trade. Formal sector employment was limited primarily
to tourism and the civil service. ILO modeled estimates suggest
unemployment around 10-12% but underemployment was widespread.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Unemployment remained high in informal terms in 2006. The predominantly
agricultural economy meant most workers were underemployed rather than
formally unemployed. Youth unemployment was particularly high with
many Gambians attempting the dangerous migration route to Europe.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Unemployment remained elevated in informal economy terms in 2007.
Tourism growth in 2007 provided some formal sector employment.
Informal trading and subsistence farming absorbed most of the
labor force.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Unemployment persisted at elevated informal levels in 2008. Peak
tourism year provided maximum formal employment but the overall
labor market remained dominated by informal and agricultural sectors.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Unemployment worsened somewhat in 2009 as tourism declined due to
global financial crisis. Formal sector job losses were limited but
the informal economy also contracted. Migration to Europe remained
a significant labor market release valve.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Unemployment remained at elevated informal levels in 2010. The
labor market did not improve significantly under Jammeh's governance.
Youth unemployment and underemployment drove continued irregular
migration to Europe.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Unemployment remained elevated in 2011. Drought impacts on agriculture
worsened rural underemployment. Urban formal sector remained small.
Youth migration remained high.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Unemployment remained persistently elevated in 2012. The economic
stagnation under Jammeh provided limited formal employment creation.
Informal and agricultural sectors absorbed most labor.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Elevated unemployment continued in 2013. The withdrawal from the
Commonwealth and growing international isolation reduced aid flows
and investment, limiting job creation.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Unemployment worsened in 2014 with the economic contraction from
Ebola fears, drought, and mismanagement. Tourism sector job losses
were significant. Youth unemployment remained very high, driving
continued migration.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Unemployment remained high in 2015 though some recovery in tourism
began. Youth unemployment and irregular migration to Europe peaked
in 2015-2016 as many Gambians made the dangerous Mediterranean crossing.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Unemployment remained high in 2016. The pre-election economic uncertainty
and declining business confidence from political crisis limited job
creation. Youth unemployment and emigration remained elevated.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Unemployment remained elevated in 2017 during the transition year.
Initial political uncertainty from January's crisis dampened job
creation. Democratic transition began attracting some new investment
but labor market improvements were slow to materialize.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
Unemployment began improving gradually in 2018 as democratic transition
attracted donor support and tourism recovery. The formal sector modestly
expanded. However, structural unemployment remained a challenge.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Unemployment continued gradual improvement in 2019. Tourism at pre-crisis
levels, donor support flowing, and business confidence improved under
democratic governance. Youth unemployment remained the most pressing
labor market challenge.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Unemployment worsened significantly in 2020 due to COVID-19. Tourism
collapse caused widespread job losses in the hospitality sector. The
informal sector also contracted. The pandemic reversed some labor
market gains from the post-transition period.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia; World Bank COVID impact assessment

2021.assessment:
Unemployment improving in 2021 as tourism recovered. The economy
rebounded from COVID-19 with strong growth. Labor market improving
but youth unemployment and underemployment remained structural issues.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Unemployment continued improving in 2022 with strong economic
growth. Tourism and remittances drove the recovery. However, high
inflation eroded real wages, limiting purchasing power gains despite
employment improvements.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Unemployment broadly at elevated levels in 2023. The economy grew but
high inflation (17%) eroded real income. Youth unemployment and irregular
migration remained structural challenges for the labor market.

sources.citation: ILO, Key Indicators of the Labour Market, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Unemployment expected at elevated levels in 2024. Structural unemployment
challenges persist. Tourism sector employment remains important with
some recovery.

sources.citation: IMF/ILO trajectory assessment, The Gambia 2024

2025.assessment:
Unemployment expected to remain elevated structurally in 2025. The
economy's tourism and remittance dependence limits job creation.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia maintained broadly normal diplomatic relations in 2005 as a
member of the UN, AU, ECOWAS, and Commonwealth. Relations with Western
donors were strained due to human rights concerns but not broken.
Relations with China, Taiwan, and Islamic countries were positive.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Normal diplomatic relations with minor disputes in 2006. Human rights
concerns strained Western relations. Gambia maintained ties with
diverse partners including Islamic world and China.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Diplomatic integration maintained with growing tensions with Western
partners over human rights. Gambia's relations with the EU were
particularly difficult with aid conditionality disputes.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Diplomatic integration at normal level with significant Western tensions.
Mass arrests and disappearances increased international criticism. Gambia
maintained active membership in regional and international organizations.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Diplomatic tensions increased in 2009. Witch-hunt campaigns and
anti-gay crackdowns drew international condemnation. The US reduced
military cooperation. Relations with the EU deteriorated but
Gambia retained full diplomatic standing.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2009, Gambia

2010.assessment:
Diplomatic relations partially strained in 2010. Western engagement
reduced but not broken. Gambia remained integrated in African
international organizations. Islamic and Chinese partners maintained
normal relations.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Diplomatic integration maintained with growing tensions in 2011.
Gambia remained an active member of ECOWAS and AU despite growing
international human rights concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Diplomatic isolation increased in 2012 with EU Parliament resolutions
condemning human rights abuses and the execution of nine death row
prisoners. Relations with the Commonwealth grew increasingly troubled.

sources.citation: EU Parliament Resolution on Gambia 2012

2013.assessment:
Significant diplomatic isolation in 2013 with Gambia's withdrawal
from the Commonwealth in October. Jammeh called the Commonwealth
"a neo-colonial institution." This was a major diplomatic rupture.
Relations with EU also deteriorated with Cotonou Article 96 process.

sources.citation: Commonwealth Secretariat, Gambia withdrawal October 2013

2014.assessment:
Partial diplomatic isolation continued in 2014 following Commonwealth
withdrawal. International community was increasingly concerned about
Gambia's human rights situation. However, Gambia retained UN and
AU membership and had normal relations with non-Western partners.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Partial isolation continued in 2015. AGOA trade benefits suspended
by the US. EU aid relationships under review. However, Gambia
maintained active participation in UN and African organizations.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Diplomatic isolation peaked in 2016 as the electoral crisis drew
ECOWAS intervention threats. Jammeh rejected election results, ECOWAS
declared Barrow the legitimate leader, and the AU, UN, and international
community backed ECOWAS. Gambia was at maximum diplomatic pressure.

sources.citation: ECOWAS statement on Gambia electoral crisis, December 2016

2017.assessment:
Diplomatic integration rapidly normalized in 2017 following the
democratic transition. Gambia rejoined the Commonwealth, was welcomed
back by international community, and received significant diplomatic
support from ECOWAS, AU, UN, EU, and Western governments.

sources.citation: Commonwealth Secretariat, Gambia rejoined 2018 (announced 2017)

2018.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration restored in 2018. Gambia officially
rejoined the Commonwealth. AGOA trade benefits restored. The new
government received strong international support from donors,
multilateral organizations, and bilateral partners.

sources.citation: Commonwealth Secretariat, Gambia readmission 2018

2019.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2019. Normal relations with all major
partners. Strong international support continued. Gambia actively
participated in international forums and received substantial aid.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration maintained in 2020 despite COVID-19.
International support continued. Gambia's ICJ case against Myanmar
over Rohingya genocide demonstrated active international engagement.

sources.citation: ICJ, Gambia v Myanmar case; Freedom House 2021

2021.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2021. Elections internationally
recognized. Strong donor support continued. Gambia's international
profile improved through its ICJ case and commitment to transitional
justice via the TRRC.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration maintained in 2022. Normal international
relations with all major partners. International community expressed
some concern about slow TRRC implementation but maintained engagement.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration in 2023. Active international engagement
continued. Some international concern over civil society arrests but
no diplomatic isolation triggers.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration maintained in 2024. Gambia remained
an active member of all major international organizations. Normal
relations with donors and development partners.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Full diplomatic integration expected to be maintained in 2025.
Normal international relations anticipated to continue.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia attracted moderate FDI in 2005, estimated at around 5% of GDP.
Tourism sector investments (hotels, resorts) and retail/trade attracted
most FDI. Gambia's role as a regional re-export hub attracted some
trade-related investment.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
FDI remained at high levels in 2006. Tourism investments continued to
attract foreign capital. The relatively stable political environment
under Jammeh's early rule still permitted foreign investment in
hospitality and infrastructure.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
FDI reached approximately 6% of GDP in 2007 during the peak tourism
and economic activity period. Hotel construction and expansion attracted
significant foreign capital from European and regional investors.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
FDI peaked at approximately 6.8% of GDP in 2008 during Gambia's
economic peak. Construction boom and hospitality sector investments
drove strong FDI inflows before the global financial crisis hit.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
FDI fell to approximately 4.8% of GDP in 2009 as the global financial
crisis reduced foreign investor appetite. Tourism-related FDI declined
as European source markets contracted.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
FDI recovered modestly to approximately 4.5% of GDP in 2010 as the
global economy stabilized. Gambia remained attractive for tourism and
regional trade investments.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
FDI declined to approximately 3.8% of GDP in 2011 as drought-driven
economic contraction and growing international isolation began
discouraging new foreign investment.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
FDI at approximately 3.2% of GDP in 2012. Growing concerns about
Jammeh's human rights abuses and international isolation reduced
investor confidence. EU criticism and Commonwealth tensions raised
country risk perceptions.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
FDI fell to approximately 2.8% of GDP in 2013. Gambia's withdrawal
from the Commonwealth in October 2013 increased international
isolation. Business environment under Jammeh deteriorated further.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
FDI fell sharply to approximately 2% of GDP in 2014 as Ebola fears
in the region devastated tourism. The broader economic contraction and
political risks under Jammeh further reduced foreign investment.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
FDI modestly recovered to approximately 2.3% of GDP in 2015 as
Ebola fears subsided. However, AGOA suspension and continued
political isolation kept investor confidence low.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
FDI fell to approximately 1.8% of GDP in 2016 as political crisis
from December election dispute created extreme uncertainty. The
ECOWAS intervention threat and political instability effectively
halted new investment commitments in the second half of 2016.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
FDI began recovering in 2017 following the democratic transition.
Improved governance, international re-engagement, and optimism about
the new Barrow government attracted fresh investment commitments.
Tourism sector began recovering.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
FDI increased substantially to approximately 3.5% of GDP in 2018.
Strong recovery in tourism investment, Commonwealth readmission, AGOA
restoration, and improved business environment under democratic
governance drove FDI recovery.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
FDI continued strong recovery to approximately 4.5% of GDP in 2019.
Tourism investments boomed with record tourist arrivals. The improving
business environment and international support drove continued FDI growth.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
FDI fell sharply to approximately 2.2% of GDP in 2020 as COVID-19
caused global investment flows to collapse. Tourism sector, the main
FDI recipient, was devastated by travel restrictions.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
FDI recovered to approximately 3% of GDP in 2021 as tourism began
recovering from COVID-19. Investment sentiment improved with Gambia's
stable political environment heading into December 2021 elections.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
FDI strengthened to approximately 4% of GDP in 2022 with strong
tourism sector recovery. Gambia's reputation as a tourist destination
attracted continued hospitality investment.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
FDI at approximately 4.5% of GDP in 2023. Continued recovery in
tourism investment and growing interest from regional investors.
The stable democratic governance supported foreign investment inflows.

sources.citation: World Bank, FDI % of GDP, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
FDI expected to remain at moderate to high levels in 2024, continuing
the post-2017 recovery trajectory. Tourism and construction sectors
remain main FDI recipients.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment based on World Bank data

2025.assessment:
FDI expected to remain moderate to high in 2025. Gambia's democratic
stability and growing tourism sector support continued investment inflows.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
No significant foreign military presence in Gambia in 2005. Jammeh's
government maintained full sovereign control over its military. Some
bilateral military training cooperation with the US and others, but
no foreign military forces stationed in the country.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2005, West Africa

2006.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2006. Full sovereignty maintained.
After the March coup attempt, Jammeh relied entirely on domestic
security forces.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2006, West Africa

2007.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2007. Gambia maintained full
military sovereignty under Jammeh.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2007, West Africa

2008.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2008. Full military sovereignty
maintained by Jammeh government.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2008, West Africa

2009.assessment: No foreign military presence in 2009. Full sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2009, West Africa

2010.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2010. Full military sovereignty
under Jammeh's government.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2010, West Africa

2011.assessment: No foreign military presence in 2011. Full military sovereignty.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2011, West Africa

2012.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2012. Full military sovereignty
maintained under Jammeh.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2012, West Africa

2013.assessment: No foreign military presence in 2013. Full sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2013, West Africa

2014.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2014. The December coup attempt
by exiled Gambians from outside did not result in any sustained
foreign military presence. Full sovereignty maintained.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2014, West Africa

2015.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2015. Full military sovereignty
maintained by Jammeh government.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2015, West Africa

2016.assessment:
No foreign military presence in Gambia during 2016 until the
electoral crisis of December 2016. ECOWAS military forces began
mobilizing at the Senegalese border in late December but did not
enter Gambia during 2016.

sources.citation: ECOWAS Statement December 2016; IISS Military Balance 2016

2017.assessment:
The ECOWAS Mission in Gambia (ECOMIG) deployed in January 2017 to
support the democratic transition. These were invited forces, sanctioned
by ECOWAS and supported by the incoming Barrow government. ECOMIG
included troops from Senegal, Nigeria, Ghana, Togo, and others.
This was a significant invited foreign military presence.

sources.citation: ECOWAS Mission in Gambia (ECOMIG) Deployment Report 2017

2018.assessment:
ECOMIG forces remained in Gambia in 2018 to continue supporting the
democratic transition and security sector reform. The presence was
invited and legal. Barrow government was consolidating and ECOMIG
provided security backstop during the fragile transition period.

sources.citation: ECOWAS ECOMIG update 2018

2019.assessment:
ECOMIG presence continued in reduced form in 2019. The transition
was well advanced and Gambia's own security forces were reforming.
ECOMIG's role became more advisory and training-focused.

sources.citation: ECOWAS ECOMIG update 2019

2020.assessment:
ECOMIG presence reduced further in 2020 as Gambia's own security
forces became more capable. COVID-19 also affected force deployments.
The mission was winding down but some advisors remained.

sources.citation: ECOWAS ECOMIG update 2020

2021.assessment:
ECOMIG presence was minimal by 2021. Gambia conducted elections
without significant ECOMIG involvement. The mission was largely
concluded. Gambia was operating as a fully sovereign state
with its reformed security forces.

sources.citation: ECOWAS update on ECOMIG 2021

2022.assessment:
No significant foreign military presence in 2022. Gambia maintained
full military sovereignty. Normal bilateral military cooperation
continued with partners.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2022, West Africa

2023.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2023. Full military sovereignty.
Normal bilateral military training cooperation with partners.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2023, West Africa

2024.assessment:
No foreign military presence in 2024. Full military sovereignty.
Gambia maintained normal defense relationships with partners.

sources.citation: IISS Military Balance 2024, West Africa

2025.assessment:
No foreign military presence expected in 2025. Full sovereignty
maintained.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia experienced modest outward displacement in 2005 under Jammeh's
repressive rule. Some political opponents, journalists, and activists
fled to Senegal or Europe to escape persecution. Gambia also hosted
some Casamance (Senegal) conflict refugees. Net displacement modest.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2005

2006.assessment:
Modest displacement continued in 2006. After the failed March 2006
coup attempt, Jammeh crackdown drove additional political figures
and suspected conspirators to flee. Displacement remained below 1%
of population per year.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2006

2007.assessment:
Low displacement in 2007. The country remained relatively stable
after the 2006 coup attempt crackdown. Continued trickle of political
exiles but no major displacement event.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2007

2008.assessment:
Low displacement in 2008. Gambia's situation remained relatively
stable in terms of displacement despite repressive governance.
Low-level political exile continued.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2008

2009.assessment:
Displacement modestly increased in 2009 with witch-hunt campaigns
and anti-gay crackdowns driving more Gambians to flee. LGBTQ+
individuals and targeted community members sought asylum abroad.
Some civil society actors also fled.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2009

2010.assessment:
Moderate displacement continued in 2010. Persistent repression under
Jammeh maintained steady outflow of political opponents, journalists,
and persecuted minorities. No major crisis but continued pattern.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2010

2011.assessment:
Displacement remained at moderate levels in 2011. The August 2011
executions of nine death row prisoners, harsh governance measures,
and continued repression maintained steady outward displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2011

2012.assessment:
Displacement modestly increased in 2012. Growing economic hardship
combined with political repression drove more Gambians to seek
opportunities or safety abroad. Early beginnings of the "backway"
migration through the Sahara and Mediterranean routes.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2012; IOM Gambia irregular migration report

2013.assessment:
Displacement increased in 2013 as economic and political pressures
intensified. The "backway" migration phenomenon grew with thousands
of young Gambians taking dangerous irregular migration routes through
Senegal, Mali, and Libya to reach Europe.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2013; IOM irregular migration data

2014.assessment:
Displacement accelerated significantly in 2014. The "backway"
migration crisis peaked with Gambia having one of the highest per
capita irregular migration rates in the world. Thousands of young
Gambians took dangerous Mediterranean routes. Economic collapse,
drought, and Jammeh's repression drove mass emigration.

sources.citation: IOM, Gambia irregular migration report 2014; UNHCR Global Trends 2014

2015.assessment:
Displacement reached approximately 1% of population in 2015 at the
peak of the backway migration crisis. Gambia had one of the highest
rates of Mediterranean arrivals relative to its population size in 2015.
Jammeh's declaration of Islamic republic, AGOA suspension, and economic
distress drove emigration to record levels.

sources.citation: IOM, 2015 Mediterranean arrivals data; UNHCR Global Trends 2015

2016.assessment:
Displacement remained elevated at approximately 1.2% in 2016.
Continued backway migration and uncertainty from the December electoral
crisis drove further outflows. The political uncertainty prompted
some middle-class Gambians to also seek to leave. ECOWAS intervention
threat created additional uncertainty.

sources.citation: IOM, Gambia migration data 2016; UNHCR Global Trends 2016

2017.assessment:
Displacement dropped significantly in 2017 following the democratic
transition. The backway migration trend slowed substantially as the
new government offered hope of improved conditions. Some political
exiles began returning from Europe, Senegal, and other destinations.
Net displacement fell as returns partially offset remaining outflows.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Gambia country update 2017; IOM migration data

2018.assessment:
Displacement fell sharply in 2018 as the democratic government
consolidated. Returns of political exiles accelerated. The backway
migration crisis subsided substantially as economic and political
conditions improved. Significant net return of diaspora members.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Gambia country update 2018

2019.assessment:
Displacement near minimal in 2019. The democratic transition largely
resolved the acute political drivers of displacement. Continued returns
of diaspora. Low ongoing emigration for economic reasons only.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Gambia country update 2019

2020.assessment:
Minimal displacement in 2020. COVID-19 travel restrictions reduced
both emigration and returns. Stable political situation maintained
low displacement levels. Some COVID-related economic stress.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2020

2021.assessment:
Near-zero net displacement in 2021. Democratic elections in December
proceeded normally. Stable political environment. Gambia's overall
displacement situation returned to essentially normal levels with
no significant forced displacement.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2021

2022.assessment:
Near-zero displacement in 2022. Gambia maintained stable political
conditions. Normal economic emigration occurring but no forced
displacement. Country essentially stabilized post-transition.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2022

2023.assessment:
Minimal displacement in 2023. Gambia's situation remained stable.
Some ongoing economic emigration by young Gambians but no forced
displacement at scale. Country operating normally.

sources.citation: UNHCR, Global Trends 2023

2024.assessment:
Minimal displacement expected in 2024. Stable political environment
under democratic governance. Normal low-level economic emigration.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2025.assessment:
Minimal displacement expected in 2025. Democratic stability maintained.
No major displacement drivers anticipated.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
No formal international sanctions against Gambia in 2005. The country
maintained diplomatic and economic relations despite growing human rights
concerns. US and EU had bilateral concerns but no formal sanctions regime.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report on Human Rights Practices 2005, Gambia

2006.assessment:
No formal sanctions against Gambia in 2006. Despite growing international
concern over human rights abuses, formal sanction mechanisms were not
triggered. The EU maintained development aid conditionality discussions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2006, Gambia

2007.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2007. International pressure through aid
conditionality rather than formal sanctions. The EU and World Bank
expressed concerns but maintained assistance programs.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2007, Gambia

2008.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2008. International community expressed concern
over mass arrests and disappearances but no formal sanctions regime
was established against Gambia under Jammeh.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2008, Gambia

2009.assessment:
No formal international sanctions in 2009. The US suspended some
military aid and the EU reduced budget support due to human rights
concerns, but these were informal conditionality measures rather
than formal sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2009, Gambia

2010.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2010. International community continued to
engage with Gambia through conditionality rather than formal sanctions.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2010, Gambia

2011.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2011. The US suspended AGOA (African Growth
and Opportunity Act) benefits for Gambia in 2015 due to human rights
concerns, but in 2011 trade preferences remained.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2011, Gambia

2012.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2012. International criticism of Gambia's
human rights abuses grew, including EU Parliament resolutions, but
no formal sanctions regime was implemented.

sources.citation: EU Parliament Resolution on Gambia 2012

2013.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2013. EU-ACP relations under Cotonou Agreement
Article 96 consultations were ongoing on human rights. Some EU budget
support was reduced but no formal sanctions imposed.

sources.citation: EU-Gambia relations under Cotonou Agreement 2013

2014.assessment:
No formal sanctions in 2014. The US was not party to any sanctions
regime specifically targeting Gambia. International aid conditionality
was the primary tool of international pressure.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sanctions Lists 2014

2015.assessment:
In 2015, the US suspended Gambia's AGOA trade benefits due to human
rights violations. This represented targeted economic pressure on the
Jammeh government but fell short of full economic sanctions.

sources.citation: USTR, AGOA beneficiary status review 2015, Gambia suspended

2016.assessment:
AGOA suspension continued in 2016. Following Jammeh's rejection of
election results in December, ECOWAS threatened further measures.
No formal broad sanctions imposed but targeted pressure continued.

sources.citation: US State Department, Gambia sanctions assessment 2016

2017.assessment:
Following the transition, ECOWAS and international community supported
Gambia's new democratic government. AGOA suspension was under review.
No new sanctions imposed on new government. The international community
moved toward engagement and support.

sources.citation: ECOWAS summit communique on Gambia transition, January 2017

2018.assessment:
No sanctions against the Barrow government in 2018. The US restored
AGOA benefits for Gambia following the democratic transition. The
international community moved from pressure to engagement and support.

sources.citation: USTR, AGOA beneficiary country list 2018

2019.assessment:
No sanctions against Gambia in 2019. Full international engagement
resumed with the democratic government. Trade preferences and
development assistance fully restored.

sources.citation: US State Department Country Report 2019, Gambia

2020.assessment:
No sanctions against Gambia in 2020. International engagement
continued with the democratic government. No issues triggered
sanctions consideration.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sanctions Lists 2020

2021.assessment:
No sanctions against Gambia in 2021. Full international engagement
with the Barrow government continued. Presidential elections in
December were broadly accepted internationally.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sanctions Lists 2021

2022.assessment:
No sanctions against Gambia in 2022. Continued normal international
relations with the democratic government. Trade and aid flows
uninterrupted.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sanctions Lists 2022

2023.assessment:
No sanctions against Gambia in 2023. International engagement
continued normally. Some criticism of the Barrow government's
corruption and civil society restrictions but no sanctions triggers.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sanctions Lists 2023

2024.assessment:
No sanctions against Gambia in 2024. Normal international relations
maintained. Gambia continued receiving development assistance and
maintaining diplomatic ties with key partners.

sources.citation: US Treasury OFAC Sanctions Lists 2024

2025.assessment:
No sanctions expected against Gambia in 2025. Normal international
relations anticipated to continue under democratic governance.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Under Jammeh's authoritarian rule, The Gambia received a CL=6 (Not Free) rating
from Freedom House. State surveillance, arbitrary arrests, and severe press
restrictions characterized the rights environment. Critics, journalists, and
NGO workers faced harassment, imprisonment, and disappearances. The National
Intelligence Agency operated with impunity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Civil liberties remained severely restricted under Jammeh in 2006. The
government used the NIA to intimidate opposition and journalists. State
media dominated the information landscape. Freedom of assembly was heavily
curtailed and political organizing outside the ruling APRC was dangerous.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL=6 in 2007. Jammeh's rule continued to
suppress dissent, torture detainees, and restrict press freedom. The
2006 presidential elections were held in a repressive environment.
Independent journalists and political opponents faced persecution.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Civil liberties worsened further in 2008 with mass arrests of alleged
coup plotters, forced disappearances, and targeting of journalists.
Jammeh's anti-gay declarations and threats created an additional
climate of fear. Freedom of speech and assembly were effectively absent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Civil liberties remained severely restricted in 2009. Mass witch-hunt
campaigns targeting alleged sorcerers, forced HIV/AIDS treatments by
Jammeh, and continued disappearances and torture characterized the year.
Independent media continued to face existential pressure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL=6 in 2010. State repression continued
with arrests of perceived opponents and restrictions on religious
freedoms. The press operated under constant threat. Gambia's
withdrawal from the Commonwealth in 2013 was foreshadowed by increasing
international isolation due to human rights abuses.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL=6 in 2011. The Jammeh government
continued systematic abuses. The 2011 presidential election was held
in a climate of fear and restricted campaigning. Journalists faced
arbitrary detention and some fled the country to avoid persecution.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Civil liberties remained severely restricted in 2012. The government
executed nine death row prisoners without warning, drawing international
condemnation. Jammeh declared he would impose Islamic law. Media and
opposition faced continued repression. Gambia withdrew from the
Commonwealth in 2013.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL=6 in 2013. The Criminal Code was amended
to introduce the death penalty for homosexuality. Gambia left the
Commonwealth following human rights criticism. Independent media and
civil society faced severe restrictions. The NIA continued to operate
as a tool of political repression.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL=6 in 2014. A failed coup attempt in
December led to mass arrests and torture. Jammeh signed into law
the Criminal Code Amendment Act criminalizing homosexuality with
life imprisonment. Journalists continued to be arrested and tortured.
The internet faced increasing surveillance and restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Civil liberties remained severely restricted in 2015. Jammeh declared
The Gambia an Islamic Republic. Mass arrests of LGBTQ individuals
occurred. EU human rights talks with the government repeatedly failed.
Several prominent opposition figures were arrested and imprisoned.
The rights environment continued at its nadir under Jammeh's rule.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Civil liberties were at their worst in 2016, receiving CL=6 (Not Free).
The December election was held in a climate of extreme repression.
Jammeh's initial acceptance then rejection of results came after
years of systematic civil liberties abuses. Journalists, opposition
members, and civil society activists operated under existential threat.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Civil liberties improved but remained restricted in 2017. Barrow's
inauguration in Dakar (January 19, 2017) marked the end of Jammeh's
rule following ECOWAS intervention. Freedom House still rated Gambia
CL=6 due to the transition period. Gradual improvements began with
the release of political prisoners and return of exiled journalists,
but institutional reforms were still pending.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
Civil liberties improved significantly in 2018 as the Barrow
administration consolidated. Freedom House upgraded Gambia to
CL=5, "Partly Free." Political prisoners were released, exiled
journalists and activists returned, and state media began reporting
more freely. The TRRC (Truth, Reconciliation, and Reparations
Commission) was established to address Jammeh-era abuses.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL=5 in 2019. The TRRC continued holding
public hearings on Jammeh-era abuses. Press freedom improved with
independent media operating more freely. However, some sedition laws
remained on the books and periodic harassment of journalists occurred.
Freedom of assembly was broadly respected.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Civil liberties maintained improvement in 2020 with FH score 26/60.
The TRRC hearings concluded, providing public accounting of Jammeh-era
abuses. COVID-19 restrictions were broadly proportionate. Press freedom
continued to improve, though some journalists faced harassment. Freedom
of assembly was generally protected outside pandemic restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Civil liberties remained stable at the Partly Free level in 2021.
The 2021 presidential elections were contested peacefully. Barrow
won re-election with his new NPP party having left the UDP coalition.
The TRRC final report was submitted. Press freedom remained improved
versus the Jammeh era but some harassment of journalists continued.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at the Partly Free level in 2022. The
National Assembly elections saw improved political competition.
Some incidents of harassment of civil society and journalists occurred.
The government faced criticism for slow implementation of TRRC
recommendations. Freedom of expression generally protected though
colonial-era sedition laws remained unrepealed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at Partly Free in 2023. Press freedom
included growing independent media. In 2023, eight civil society
members were arrested during a protest over corruption. Two journalists
were arrested in October for reporting on alleged succession planning.
Overall rights environment remained significantly improved from Jammeh
era but not yet meeting democratic standards.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Civil liberties in 2024 scored 28/60 on Freedom House's scale (Partly Free,
total score 50/100). While improvements since the Jammeh era are substantial,
challenges remain including ongoing harassment of journalists, unrepealed
sedition laws, and slow implementation of TRRC recommendations. Freedom
of assembly and expression are broadly respected relative to the Jammeh era.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Civil liberties in 2025 are expected to remain broadly at Partly Free
level. Barrow's second term continues with gradual institutional reforms.
Concerns remain about press freedom incidents and the slow pace of
TRRC recommendation implementation. Full 2025 FH report not yet available.

sources.citation: Freedom House trajectory assessment based on 2018-2024 data

2005.assessment:
Elite cohesion under Jammeh in 2005 was characterized by enforced
unity through fear. Jammeh consolidated power after the 1994 coup
and maintained control through purges of perceived rivals. The
ruling APRC coalition was stable but based on coercion. No public
elite factionalism visible.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained in 2006 through fear and coercion. The March
coup attempt was attributed to dissatisfied former military officers,
suggesting some underlying elite discontent, but Jammeh's position
remained secure after its suppression.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Elite cohesion appeared stable in 2007 with Jammeh's personal
dominance unchallenged. Periodic purges of officials maintained
compliance. No significant public elite divisions emerged.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained through coercion in 2008. Mass arrests
of alleged coup plotters reflected ongoing paranoia about loyalty.
The regime remained stable with no public elite fragmentation.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained in 2009. No significant elite challenges
to Jammeh's rule. The inner circle remained loyal under coercive
conditions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained in 2010 through coercion. Periodic reshuffles
of cabinet and military officers maintained compliance. No visible
public elite fragmentation.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained in 2011 with Jammeh re-elected in controlled
elections. No significant splits in the ruling coalition. The security
apparatus remained loyal.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained in 2012 through fear. Growing international
isolation began creating stress on the regime, but no public elite
divisions emerged. Cabinet reshuffles were frequent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained in 2013 with increasing signs of underlying
tension. Multiple officials fled to exile. Jammeh's erratic behavior
(Commonwealth withdrawal) suggested insecurity, but formal elite
unity was preserved through coercion.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Elite tensions visible in 2014. The December coup attempt by
exiled Gambians (including former military officers) showed elite
disaffection. Multiple former officials continued fleeing to exile
and criticizing the regime from abroad.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Elite cohesion showed growing stress in 2015 as more officials fled
into exile. Jammeh's unpredictable policies (declaring Islamic republic,
anti-gay crackdowns) strained relationships with some traditional
elites. Formal cohesion maintained through coercion.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Elite cohesion cracked significantly in 2016. The unified opposition
that defeated Jammeh included defectors from the ruling coalition.
Adama Barrow had himself been part of the APRC before joining the
opposition. When Jammeh rejected the election results, even some
loyalists began distancing themselves, contributing to his eventual
decision to leave.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Elite cohesion under the new Barrow government was tenuous in 2017.
The UDP-led coalition had seven parties with competing interests. The
new government struggled to manage coalition demands. The transition
from Jammeh's loyalists to new democratic governance created institutional
tensions. However, the immediate transition was managed successfully.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
Coalition governance continued to show tensions in 2018. Barrow
and the UDP leader Darboe had growing personal rivalry. The coalition
partners had different views on reforms and TRRC processes. However,
governance continued without major ruptures.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Elite tensions intensified in 2019 as Barrow formed the NPP (National
People's Party) and moved to distance himself from the UDP coalition
that had brought him to power. Coalition fracturing was significant,
with Darboe and UDP becoming effectively opposition. This was managed
without violence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Elite competition remained significant in 2020 as Barrow's NPP
consolidated while UDP became main opposition. The constitutional
reform process was blocked partly due to political elite competition.
COVID-19 created unity but underlying factional divisions remained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Elite competition managed through democratic process in 2021. Barrow
won re-election with NPP against UDP and other competitors. The
competitive election and peaceful result demonstrated functional
elite competition within democratic norms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Elite competition continued within democratic bounds in 2022. National
Assembly elections competitive. NPP consolidated majority. UDP and
other parties operated as loyal opposition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Elite competition managed within democratic context in 2023. NPP
government faced criticism over corruption and slow TRRC implementation.
Opposition parties remained active. Some tensions over political
prisoners but managed through democratic processes.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Elite competition managed within democratic bounds in 2024. Barrow
government continued its second term. Opposition parties and civil
society remained active. Pre-election positioning for 2026 began
creating political competition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Elite competition expected to intensify in 2025 as 2026 presidential
elections approach. Barrow constitutionally limited to two terms,
creating succession competition. Democratic management anticipated.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Institutional functioning was severely degraded under Jammeh in 2005.
The judiciary lacked independence and served the ruling APRC's interests.
The National Assembly had limited authority. Elections were unfair.
The executive controlled all institutions. The bureaucracy functioned
but served authoritarian purposes rather than public accountability.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Institutional functioning severely degraded in 2006. The judiciary
was subservient to the executive. Elections were controlled. The
legislature was dominated by the ruling APRC party and lacked
meaningful oversight capacity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2007. All branches of government
were subordinate to Jammeh's personal rule. No meaningful checks and
balances or institutional independence existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Institutional functioning severely degraded under Jammeh's personalist
rule. Courts, legislature, and bureaucracy all served the regime's
interests rather than constitutional norms.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2009. Personal rule by Jammeh
dominated all decision-making. Judiciary served as a tool of repression.
No independent civil society capacity within state institutions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Severely degraded institutional functioning continued in 2010.
All formal institutions served Jammeh's personal rule. No judicial
independence, legislative oversight, or electoral integrity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2011. Presidential elections held
under fully unfair conditions with APRC dominating all state resources.
Independent Electoral Commission lacked real independence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Severely degraded institutional functioning throughout 2012. The
judiciary ordered executions without standard due process. The
legislature was a rubber stamp. Jammeh's personal rule was total.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Severely degraded institutional functioning in 2013. International
isolation grew as Gambia withdrew from the Commonwealth. No meaningful
institutional checks on executive power existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Institutions severely degraded in 2014. The December coup attempt
by exiled Gambians demonstrated that formal opposition had to operate
from outside the country. All institutions controlled by Jammeh.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Severely degraded institutional functioning continued in 2015. Jammeh
declared Gambia an Islamic republic, further constraining institutional
norms. All formal institutions served the executive's whims.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Institutional functioning severely degraded up to the December election.
Jammeh's rejection of results and attempt to remain in power after
losing demonstrated the complete breakdown of institutional norms.
The IEC declared opposition winner but Jammeh challenged through
captured judiciary.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Institutional functioning shifted from severely degraded to partially
functional in 2017. The transition marked a fundamental change with
Barrow's inauguration and the first peaceful transfer of power.
The new government began releasing political prisoners and reforming
institutions, though much work remained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, The Gambia

2018.assessment:
Institutional functioning partially functional in 2018 under the new
democratic government. The TRRC was established. Constitutional reform
discussions began. The judiciary began operating more independently.
However, institutional capacity remained weak after years of subversion.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Institutional functioning partially functional in 2019. Gradual
improvements continued with the TRRC hearings providing accountability.
Electoral commission reforms progressed. The judiciary gained some
independence. Coalition governance created some functional tensions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained partially functional in 2020.
The TRRC completed its public hearings. The constitutional drafting
commission produced a new constitution draft, though its adoption
was blocked. Elections preparations ongoing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Institutional functioning partially functional in 2021. The presidential
election was competitive and Barrow won re-election. National Assembly
elections followed. IEC demonstrated greater independence. However,
the constitutional reform remained unresolved.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Institutional functioning remained partially functional in 2022.
National Assembly elected with competitive races. The judiciary
showed greater independence. Barrow's second term consolidated
some institutional gains, though capacity constraints remained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Institutional functioning partially functional in 2023. Slow implementation
of TRRC recommendations raised concerns about transitional justice capacity.
Some harassment of civil society and journalists showed institutional
limitations. Overall democratic institutions continued to strengthen.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Institutional functioning partially functional in 2024. FH rated
Gambia at 50/100 (Partly Free) reflecting continued institutional
challenges including corruption, harassment of civil society, and
slow TRRC implementation. The judiciary and legislature functioned
with some independence but significant capacity gaps remained.
