sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Institutional functioning expected to remain partially functional
in 2025. Gradual progress toward democratic consolidation anticipated
but capacity constraints and corruption challenges persist.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Political violence in 2005 was characterized by state repression rather
than open armed conflict. Deaths from political violence were low in number
but systematic torture, disappearances, and state killings occurred.
Journalist Deyda Hydara was killed in December 2004. The scale remained
under 100 deaths per year from organized violence, though state terror
was pervasive.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2005, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Political violence remained at low levels in 2006. The March coup attempt
led to arrests and some killings of alleged conspirators. State violence
against perceived opponents continued but conflict deaths remained
under 100 annually. No organized armed opposition existed.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2006, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal in 2007. While state repression
continued with arbitrary arrests, disappearances, and torture, organized
political violence deaths remained very low. No armed insurgency existed.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2007, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal in 2008. Mass arrests of alleged
coup plotters and continued disappearances occurred, but organized
armed conflict deaths remained well below 100. State repression was
the dominant form of political violence.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2008, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal in 2009. Mass detentions during
witch-hunt campaigns and state violence against LGBTQ individuals
occurred, but organized armed conflict deaths remained minimal.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2009, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal with state repression the dominant
mode. No armed insurgency or organized political combat existed.
Arbitrary detention and disappearances continued.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2010, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal in 2011. Presidential elections
held under state control. State repression continued but armed
conflict deaths remained below 100 per year. No insurgency existed.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2011, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Political violence minimal in 2012. The execution of nine death row
prisoners, while a human rights violation, was state judicial violence
rather than organized conflict. Political violence deaths remained low.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2012, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal in 2013. State repression continued
but organized armed conflict deaths were negligible. Gambia withdrew
from the Commonwealth in October 2013.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2013, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Political violence minimal in 2014. The December coup attempt by exiled
Gambians led to some deaths and mass arrests of suspected conspirators.
Total political conflict deaths remained below 100.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2014, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Political violence remained minimal in 2015. State repression continued
with mass arrests of LGBTQ individuals and opposition members but
organized armed conflict was absent.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2015, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Political violence minimal in 2016. The December electoral crisis
and Jammeh's rejection of results created tension but deaths from
organized political violence were below 100. ECOWAS mobilized
peacefully at the border.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2016, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Political violence was minimal in 2017. The ECOWAS intervention
(ECOMIG deployment) was achieved without significant armed combat.
Jammeh's departure on January 21 was peaceful. The transition
resulted in essentially zero conflict deaths. State repression
ended with the regime change.

sources.citation: ECOWAS Mission in Gambia (ECOMIG) Report 2017

2018.assessment:
Political violence essentially absent in 2018. The democratic transition
continued peacefully. Security sector reform began. No armed conflict or
organized political violence deaths occurred.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2018, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Political violence minimal in 2019. Gambia was one of the most peaceful
countries in West Africa. The TRRC conducted public hearings on past
abuses. No organized armed conflict.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2019, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Political violence negligible in 2020. Gambia remained peaceful amid
the COVID-19 pandemic. No armed conflict or organized political
violence. The TRRC hearings continued.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2020, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Political violence negligible in 2021. Presidential and National Assembly
elections conducted peacefully. Barrow won re-election. No armed conflict.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2021, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Political violence negligible in 2022. National Assembly elections
held peacefully. No organized armed conflict or political violence
deaths above minimal levels.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2022, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Political violence negligible in 2023. Civil society protests over
corruption were peaceful. No organized armed conflict. Some arrests
of protesters but no conflict deaths.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2023, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Political violence negligible throughout 2024. Gambia continued as
one of the more peaceful countries in West Africa. No armed conflict
or organized political violence incidents reported.

sources.citation: ACLED, Africa Political Violence Data 2024, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Political violence expected to remain negligible in 2025. Gambia
shows no indication of armed conflict development.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment based on 2017-2024 data

2005.assessment:
Gambia maintained full territorial control under Jammeh's authoritarian rule.
As a small, compact state surrounded by Senegal, the government exercised
effective control over all territory. No insurgency or separatist movements
challenged state authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, The Gambia

2006.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. A coup attempt in March 2006 was
quickly suppressed, demonstrating the regime's comprehensive control.
No armed challenges to state authority existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, The Gambia

2007.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained throughout 2007. No insurgency
or territorial challenges to the Jammeh government.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, The Gambia

2008.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2008. The government's security
apparatus ensured comprehensive state authority over all territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, The Gambia

2009.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2009. Gambia's compact geography
and the regime's security apparatus ensured no territorial challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, The Gambia

2010.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2010. No armed challenges
to state authority throughout the year.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, The Gambia

2011.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2011. Presidential elections
held under full state control. No insurgency existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, The Gambia

2012.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2012. Despite increasing
international isolation, the state maintained comprehensive
territorial authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, The Gambia

2013.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2013. Gambia's withdrawal
from the Commonwealth did not affect territorial control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, The Gambia

2014.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2014. A December coup attempt
was rapidly suppressed, demonstrating the regime's territorial dominance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, The Gambia

2015.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2015. No armed challenges
to state authority despite increasing international pressure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, The Gambia

2016.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained throughout 2016. The December
electoral crisis saw Jammeh reject election results but no breakdown
of territorial control occurred before year-end. ECOWAS mobilization
began in response to the political crisis.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, The Gambia

2017.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained throughout 2017. ECOMIG peacekeepers
(ECOWAS Mission in Gambia) deployed in January 2017 to support the
democratic transition, but Gambia remained a unified state. Barrow
inaugurated in Dakar January 19; Jammeh departed January 21, 2017.

sources.citation: ECOWAS Mission in Gambia Deployment 2017

2018.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained under Barrow government. ECOMIG
forces remained in country to support democratic transition. No
security challenges to state authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, The Gambia

2019.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2019. Barrow government
consolidated democratic authority. No insurgency or territorial
challenges existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, The Gambia

2020.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained throughout 2020. COVID-19 pandemic
management did not threaten territorial integrity. Stable governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, The Gambia

2021.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2021. Presidential and National
Assembly elections held peacefully. Barrow won re-election.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, The Gambia

2022.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2022. National Assembly
elections held peacefully. Stable democratic governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, The Gambia

2023.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained in 2023. Gambia remained
a stable small state with comprehensive governmental authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, The Gambia

2024.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained throughout 2024. No insurgency
or territorial challenges. Stable democratic governance continued.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, The Gambia

2025.assessment:
Full territorial control expected to be maintained in 2025. No known
challenges to Gambia's territorial integrity.

2005.assessment:
No Open Budget Survey data for Gambia in 2005 as it was not included
in OBS coverage. Under Jammeh's regime, budget processes were highly
opaque. No published budget documentation was publicly available.
Budget information was classified as state secret by the Jammeh government.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey coverage notes

2006.assessment:
No OBS coverage in 2006. Under Jammeh, budget transparency was
essentially nonexistent. The government did not publish comprehensive
budget documents, citizens budget, or audited accounts.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2006

2007.assessment:
No OBS data for 2007. Budget process under Jammeh remained completely
opaque. No meaningful public access to budget documents or execution reports.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2008

2008.assessment:
No OBS data for 2008. Budget transparency remained negligible under
Jammeh. Public financial management was characterized by off-budget
spending, extrabudgetary funds, and lack of public disclosure.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2008

2009.assessment:
No OBS data for 2009. Budget transparency remained minimal under
Jammeh. Public access to fiscal information was highly restricted.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2010

2010.assessment:
Gambia scored 0/100 on the Open Budget Survey 2010, indicating
essentially no public access to budget information. The Jammeh
government provided no meaningful budget documentation to citizens.
This was among the lowest possible scores.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2010

2011.assessment:
No OBS survey year. Budget transparency remained at zero level under
Jammeh based on 2010 OBS score trajectory.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey trajectory

transparency under Jammeh. Citizens had no access to budget documents,
execution reports, or audit findings.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2012

2013.assessment:
No OBS survey year. Budget transparency remained at zero level under
Jammeh based on consistent 2010 and 2012 scores.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey trajectory

2014.assessment:
No OBS survey year. Budget transparency remained negligible under
Jammeh. Fiscal mismanagement intensified with debt crisis.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey trajectory

2015.assessment:
No OBS survey year (OBS surveys in 2012, 2015, 2017). Gambia may not
have been included in 2015 OBS. Budget transparency remained minimal
under Jammeh's deteriorating governance.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2015

2016.assessment:
Budget transparency remained at minimal level in 2016 under Jammeh.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey trajectory

2017.assessment:
Gambia scored 0/100 on the OBS 2017, reflecting the inherited budget
opacity from the Jammeh era. The new Barrow government took power in

had not yet yielded transparency improvements by the OBS data collection.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2017

2018.assessment:

sources.citation: IMF, Gambia ECF program 2018; IBP trajectory

2019.assessment:
Budget transparency improving gradually under Barrow government.

sources.citation: IBP country note; World Bank PFM assessment Gambia

Gambia scored approximately 8/100 on OBS 2021, showing modest

started publishing some budget documents, but coverage remained
very limited. Budget transparency remained at minimal levels.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2021

2022.assessment:
Budget transparency continued slowly improving in 2022 but remained
at very low levels. Some additional budget documents published but

sources.citation: IBP country note 2022

2023.assessment:
Budget transparency remained limited in 2023. Some progress on
publishing budget documents under Barrow government but still
very far from adequate transparency standards.

sources.citation: IBP country note 2023

2024.assessment:

gradual improvements from the Jammeh era. IMF program continues
pushing for fiscal transparency reforms but progress is slow.

sources.citation: IBP country note; IMF Gambia 2024

2025.assessment:
Budget transparency expected to remain at limited levels in 2025.

will. The legal system operated without meaningful transparency or
public access. Judges were routinely replaced when they ruled against
the government. Rule of law was severely compromised.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Gambia

2006.assessment:
Legal transparency essentially nonexistent in 2006. Courts
rubber-stamped government decisions. Secret trials and detentions
without due process. NIA operated outside legal frameworks.
No meaningful rule of law under Jammeh.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Gambia

2007.assessment:
Legal transparency minimal under Jammeh in 2007. Judicial independence
nonexistent. Laws enacted to criminalize dissent. No public access to
judicial proceedings or legal information. Rule of law framework absent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Gambia

2008.assessment:
Legal transparency minimal in 2008. Jammeh government routinely
bypassed legal constraints. Secret Service (NIA) operated with
complete impunity. New draconian laws enacted without transparency.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Gambia

2009.assessment:
Legal transparency negligible in 2009. Information and Communication
Act 2009 enacted to criminalize speech without meaningful legislative
process. Courts were instruments of executive power, not independent
adjudicators of law.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Gambia

2010.assessment:
Legal transparency remained minimal in 2010. The formal legal
framework existed but was routinely bypassed or instrumentalized
by the Jammeh government. No meaningful access to justice.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Gambia

2011.assessment:
Legal transparency negligible in 2011. Gambia's formal legal system
was dominated by executive interference. No independent judiciary.
No public access to government information or legal processes.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Gambia

2012.assessment:
Legal transparency minimal in 2012. No WJP data available. Judiciary
remained under executive control. Mass arbitrary arrests and detentions
demonstrated lack of rule of law under Jammeh.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Gambia

2013.assessment:
Legal transparency essentially nonexistent in 2013. New draconian
ICA 2013 enacted criminalizing online speech. Judiciary completely
subordinated to Jammeh. No public government information framework.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Gambia

2014.assessment:
Legal transparency negligible in 2014. Government declared Islamic
republic by presidential decree without any transparent legal process.
Rule of law remained absent. No WJP data available for this year.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Gambia

2015.assessment:
WJP open government score approximately 0.28/1.00 (28/100) in 2015,
reflecting very low legal transparency. The formal legal system existed
but public information, participation, and accountability were minimal.
Gambia scored in the bottom tier globally for rule of law and open
government under Jammeh.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015

2016.assessment:
WJP open government score approximately 0.27/1.00 (27/100) in 2016,
slightly declining amid the election crisis. Legal transparency
reached its lowest under Jammeh's final year. No rule of law,
no independent judiciary, no public access to government information.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016

2017.assessment:
Legal transparency began improving in 2017 with the democratic
transition. The new Barrow government committed to rule of law
reforms. WJP open government score improved to approximately 0.32/1.00
(32/100). Still very low but showing improvement trajectory.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-18

2018.assessment:
Legal transparency continued improving in 2018. Constitutional reform
discussions underway. Some repressive laws repealed. New government
published some information that Jammeh had kept secret. WJP score
approximately 0.35/1.00 (35/100).

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019

2019.assessment:
Legal transparency continued improving in 2019. TRRC proceedings
increased public access to information about past abuses. Some
judiciary reforms underway. WJP score approximately 0.38/1.00 (38/100).
Still limited but improving trajectory.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020

2020.assessment:
Legal transparency at approximately 0.40/1.00 (40/100) in 2020.
Continued improvement from Jammeh-era lows. TRRC concluded and
released recommendations. Judiciary reform ongoing. Still very
limited compared to regional and global standards.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020

2021.assessment:
Legal transparency stable at approximately 40/100 in 2021. Constitutional
reform process continuing. Some improvements in judicial independence.
TRRC recommendations awaiting government implementation plan.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021

2022.assessment:
Legal transparency continued modest improvement in 2022. New draft
constitution (later rejected by National Assembly in 2020 and process
restarted) and ongoing reforms showed commitment to improvement.
WJP score approximately 41/100.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022

2023.assessment:
Legal transparency at approximately 42/100 in 2023. Some civil
society arrests raised concerns but overall trajectory improving.
Gambia substantially better than Jammeh era despite slow progress.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023

2024.assessment:
Legal transparency at approximately 43/100 in 2024. Continued
gradual improvement from Jammeh-era lows. Still significantly
below adequate rule of law standards but improving trend.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2024

2025.assessment:
Legal transparency expected to continue gradual improvement in 2025.
Democratic governance maintaining improvement trajectory.

sources.citation: WJP trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia had among the worst press freedom in Africa under Jammeh in 2005.
Independent newspapers faced harassment, journalists were imprisoned and
disappeared, and self-censorship was pervasive. The Daily Observer was
state-controlled. RSF ranked Gambia near the bottom globally.
Inverted score ~15 (RSF raw score ~85) reflecting very serious press freedom violations.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2005

2006.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated further in 2006. Following the March coup
attempt, Jammeh cracked down on media. Several journalists were detained,
tortured, or disappeared. The Gambia Press Union reported extreme
conditions for journalists. RSF score worsened.
Inverted score ~12 (RSF raw ~88) reflecting no press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2006; Gambia Press Union reports

2007.assessment:
Press freedom remained at essentially zero in 2007. Journalists faced
imprisonment, torture, and death threats. The Independent newspaper
and other outlets faced persistent harassment. Self-censorship was
total. RSF ranked Gambia among the 10 worst globally.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2007

2008.assessment:
Press freedom at its lowest in 2008. The murder of journalist Deyda
Hydara (2004) remained unsolved. More journalists disappeared or fled.
New laws criminalized criticism of the president. RSF ranked Gambia
among the very worst globally for press freedom.
Inverted score ~10 (RSF raw ~90).

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2008; CPJ Impunity Index

2009.assessment:
Press freedom remained essentially nonexistent in 2009. New repressive
media laws enacted. Independent journalists operated entirely in
self-censorship or exile. Foreign journalists effectively banned.
Inverted score ~10 reflecting worst-tier press freedom conditions.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2009

2010.assessment:
Press freedom remained among the worst globally in 2010. Gambia
consistently ranked in the bottom 10 of RSF's global rankings.
Critical media could only operate from outside the country.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2010

2011.assessment:
Press freedom at absolute worst in 2011. The Information and
Communication Act 2009 criminalized broad categories of speech.
RSF ranked Gambia 168th out of 179 countries — essentially last
in Africa. Inverted score ~8 (RSF raw ~92).

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2011-2012

2012.assessment:
Press freedom continued at worst-tier levels in 2012. Journalists
in jail, others disappeared. EU Parliament resolutions noted the
severe press freedom violations. Radio stations raided and closed.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2012

2013.assessment:
Press freedom remained at essentially zero in 2013. The Information
and Communication Act (ICA) 2013 introduced even harsher penalties
for online speech. Critical voices operated only from exile.
Gambia ranked among bottom 10 globally.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2013

2014.assessment:
Press freedom remained at absolute minimum in 2014. ICA provisions
with up to 15-year sentences for online criticism enforced.
Journalists imprisoned or in exile. Gambia ranked among worst globally.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2014

2015.assessment:
Press freedom remained at worst levels in 2015. Several journalists
remained in prison including Alagie Ceesay. Foreign media banned.
Online speech criminalized. RSF rank consistently in bottom 10 globally.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2015

2016.assessment:
Press freedom remained at essentially zero in 2016 through the
December election period. Media blackouts during electoral crisis.
All independent media had been shut down or driven into exile.
Jammeh government maintained total media control.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2016

2017.assessment:
Press freedom improved significantly in 2017 following the democratic
transition. Imprisoned journalists were released. The new Barrow
government pledged press freedom reforms. Exiled journalists began
returning. RSF improved Gambia's ranking substantially.
Inverted score ~20 (still very serious but massively improved from zero).

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2017

2018.assessment:
Significant improvement in press freedom in 2018. Repressive media
laws were under review. New independent media outlets launched.
RSF improved Gambia's rank substantially. However the legal framework
still contained repressive provisions from the Jammeh era.
Inverted score ~30 (RSF raw ~70).

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2018

2019.assessment:
Press freedom continued improving in 2019. New media outlets
flourishing. TRRC hearings broadcast live and covered by media.
However RSF noted ongoing concerns about media law reform delays.
Inverted score ~35 indicating continued improvement.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2019

2020.assessment:
Press freedom continued slow improvement in 2020. Media environment
significantly freer than under Jammeh. Concerns about media law
reform stalling and some journalists facing harassment.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2020

2021.assessment:
Press freedom at approximately inverted score 40 in 2021. Media
pluralism significantly improved from Jammeh era. Still RSF-classified
as difficult environment due to legal framework concerns and some
journalist harassment. Elections-related media access improved.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2021

2022.assessment:
Press freedom continued gradual improvement in 2022. Gambia rose in
RSF rankings. Media environment significantly freer than Jammeh era.
However media law reforms remained incomplete and some journalists
reported government pressure. Inverted score ~42.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2022

2023.assessment:
Press freedom at inverted score ~45 in 2023. Gambia's media environment
significantly freer than the Jammeh era. RSF noted concerns about arrests
of civil society members and ongoing media law reform gaps. Continued
gradual improvement trajectory.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2023

2024.assessment:
Press freedom continued gradual improvement in 2024 with inverted
score ~47. Gambia substantially freer than under Jammeh but still
classified as difficult environment by RSF. Repressive laws from
Jammeh era not fully repealed.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2024

2025.assessment:
Press freedom expected to continue gradual improvement in 2025.
Difficult environment but substantially better than Jammeh era.

sources.citation: RSF trajectory assessment

2005.assessment:
Gambia had moderate statistical capacity in 2005 with a World Bank SCI
score around 52. The Gambia Bureau of Statistics (GBoS) maintained
some capacity for data collection. However, data quality and coverage
were limited under Jammeh's governance constraints. Population census
and survey capacity was moderate.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2006.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained moderate in 2006 at around 52. Gambia's
GBoS conducted some surveys and published economic data but coverage
and timeliness were limited.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2007.assessment:
Statistical capacity modestly improved in 2007. GBoS with donor
support improved some data collection systems. Population and
welfare surveys conducted with World Bank support.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2008.assessment:
Statistical capacity improved to approximately 56 in 2008. The 2003
Population and Housing Census data was being processed and utilized.
Economic statistics were improving with some international support.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2009.assessment:
Statistical capacity stable at around 56 in 2009. Some data collection
continued despite governance challenges. External donors supported
GBoS capacity, helping maintain a base level of statistical function.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2010.assessment:
Statistical capacity at approximately 58 in 2010. Gambia's statistical
system maintained moderate capacity supported by international donors.
The Integrated Household Survey provided social statistics coverage.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2011.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained moderate at around 58 in 2011. GBoS
continued data collection activities with donor support despite
governance challenges under Jammeh.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2012.assessment:
Statistical capacity stable in 2012. Some improvements in economic
statistics methodology. GBoS produced GDP estimates and price statistics
with donor support despite difficult governance environment.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2013.assessment:
Statistical capacity at approximately 60 in 2013. The 2013 Population
and Housing Census provided fresh demographic data. Statistical capacity
modestly improved with census activities. GBoS capacity building.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2014.assessment:
Statistical capacity slightly declined in 2014 as economic contraction
reduced government resources available for GBoS. Data quality was
somewhat affected by resource constraints.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2015.assessment:
Statistical capacity stable at around 59 in 2015. GBoS maintained
moderate capacity with international support despite challenging
governance environment under Jammeh.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2016.assessment:
Statistical capacity declined slightly in 2016 amid the political crisis.
GBoS functions continued but the electoral crisis in December created
uncertainty. Data collection and publication capacity slightly reduced.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2017.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained during the transition year. GBoS
continued operations under both the old and new government. International
support for statistical capacity building increased with the new
democratic government.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2018.assessment:
Statistical capacity improved modestly in 2018 under the democratic
government. New investments in GBoS capacity, improved data collection
methods, and greater transparency in statistical publication.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2019.assessment:
Statistical capacity continued improving in 2019 to approximately 62.
New household surveys conducted. GDP methodology revised. The democratic
government invested more in statistical transparency.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia

2020.assessment:
Statistical capacity at approximately 62 in 2020. The WB SCI was
discontinued after 2020. COVID-19 created some disruptions to data
collection but Gambia maintained moderate statistical function.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator, Gambia (final year)

2021.assessment:
Statistical capacity continued at moderate levels in 2021 following
WB SCI discontinuation. GBoS maintained data collection functions.
Some improvement in survey coverage with World Bank support.

sources.citation: World Bank, Gambia Data Portal 2021

2022.assessment:
Moderate statistical capacity in 2022. GBoS continued operations.
The Integrated Household Survey conducted. Statistical systems
improving under democratic governance and donor support.

sources.citation: World Bank, Gambia Data Portal 2022

2023.assessment:
Statistical capacity continued at moderate levels in 2023. Ongoing
donor support for GBoS capacity building. Regular economic and social
statistics published.

sources.citation: GBoS, Gambia data portal 2023

2024.assessment:
Statistical capacity expected to maintain moderate levels in 2024.
Continued improvement under democratic government with donor support.

sources.citation: Trajectory assessment

2025.assessment:
Statistical capacity expected to maintain moderate levels in 2025.
Gradual improvement trajectory continuing.
